cues.x: Never Split the Difference, by Chris Voss.
Picture this.
You're settling in for a long drive, and I want to tell you about a book that will quietly change how you talk to every human being for the rest of your life.
Not in a manipulative way.
In a way that makes you finally, genuinely good at getting what you want while the other person feels heard, respected, even grateful.
The author is Chris Voss, and he isn't some business school professor who dreamed up theories in a quiet office.
He spent twenty-four years in the FBI, and for a big stretch of that he was the Bureau's lead international kidnapping negotiator.
His whole career was talking to people on the worst day of their lives.
Bank robbers with hostages.
Terrorists.
Kidnappers holding a family's child for ransom.
People whose next decision might kill someone.

And here's the thing that makes this book so powerful: the exact same tools he used to talk a man off a ledge or save a hostage in Manila are the tools you can use to get a raise, buy a car, close a deal, or get your kid to go to bed.
Because underneath it all, a negotiation is a negotiation.
High stakes just strips away the polite fiction and shows you what actually works.
So let's start with the title, because it's a rebellion.
Never split the difference.
Most of us grew up believing that compromise is the noble path.
Meet in the middle.
Be reasonable.
And Voss says, no, that's often the worst thing you can do.
He gives you this image that will stick in your head forever.
Imagine your husband wants to wear the black shoes with his suit.
You think he should wear the brown shoes.
So you compromise, you split the difference, and what does he end up wearing?
One black shoe and one brown shoe.
That's compromise.
It's not the best of both worlds.
It's usually the worst of both, a bad deal that nobody actually wanted, reached only because both people were too afraid or too lazy to keep going.
People split the difference because it feels safe and it feels fair, but a mediocre outcome that leaves value on the table is not a win.
Voss wants you to hold out for something better than the middle, and he's going to show you how.
Now to understand why his whole approach is so different, you have to understand what he was rebelling against.
For decades, the bible of negotiation was a book called Getting to Yes, out of the Harvard Negotiation Project.
Great book, hugely influential, and its core idea is that human beings are basically rational actors.

The theory goes: set your emotions aside, focus on the underlying interests rather than the surface positions, find mutual gains, insist on objective criteria, and two logical people will problem-solve their way to a fair deal.
And Voss was trained on that thinking.
But then he found himself sitting across from people to whom that logic was completely useless.
You cannot tell a suicidal man on a bridge to focus on his objective interests.
You cannot hand a bank robber a set of rational criteria.
What Voss learned, through blood and fear and real consequences, is that people are not rational.
We are emotional.
We're driven by fear, by the need for control, by the desire to feel understood, by the terror of loss.
And crucially, our emotions don't get in the way of the deal.
They are the deal.
The emotion is the pathway, not the obstacle.

Around the same time, behavioral economists like Daniel Kahneman were proving in laboratories what Voss was learning on rooftops: humans are riddled with cognitive biases, we feel losses about twice as intensely as equivalent gains, and the animal, emotional part of the brain runs the show far more than we like to admit.
So Voss's negotiation isn't a battle of arguments to be won with logic.
It's a process of discovery.
Your job is not to convince.
Your job is to uncover.
To find out what the other side actually needs, what they're afraid of, what they're not telling you, and often what they don't even know about themselves.
Now Voss tells a wonderful story about the moment this really crystallized for him.
He'd talked his way into a negotiation course at Harvard Law School, this FBI agent surrounded by brilliant lawyers and executives, feeling like the guy who snuck in the back door.
And in a role-play exercise, he ended up in a one-on-one with Robert Mnookin himself, a legendary Harvard professor, director of their negotiation program, a man who wrote books on the subject.
Mnookin is playing a role, coming at Voss hard, aggressive, throwing arguments at him.
And Voss, this street guy with none of the academic firepower, doesn't try to out-argue him.
He just slows everything down and asks, in that calm, warm voice, "How am I supposed to do that?"
And Mnookin answers, and Voss just listens, and asks another gentle open-ended question, and another.

And the great professor, the world expert, finds himself getting flustered, actually a little stuck, because he can't argue against a guy who isn't arguing back, who's just calmly turning every demand back into a question the professor now has to solve.
It was a demonstration in front of everyone that these FBI tools, born in life-and-death crises, were more powerful than the elegant academic theory.
And that little question, "How am I supposed to do that?", is going to come back later as one of the most valuable moves in the whole book.
So let's get into the actual system, technique by technique, because Voss builds it like a toolkit, and I want you to be able to picture using each one.
It all begins with what he calls tactical empathy.
Now empathy is a soft word, it sounds like sympathy, like feeling sorry for someone or agreeing with them.
That's not it.

Tactical empathy is the deliberate, almost surgical practice of understanding the feelings and mindset of the other person in the moment, and just as importantly, hearing what's behind those feelings, so you can influence what happens next.
It's understanding without necessarily agreeing.
You can have deep tactical empathy for a hostage-taker without approving of a single thing he's done.

It's paying such close attention to the other person's emotional world that you can name what they're feeling before they do, and that act of being truly seen is disarming in a way that nothing else is.
Every single technique that follows is really just a tool for delivering tactical empathy.
The first practical tool, and it's almost embarrassingly simple, is mirroring.
In the FBI, they sometimes call it isopraxism.
Here's the whole technique: you repeat the last one to three words the person just said.
That's it.
Somebody says, "I'm just really frustrated because the project keeps getting delayed," and you say, "The project keeps getting delayed?"
And you say it with that gentle, curious, upward inflection, like a soft question.
And what happens is almost magical.
The other person elaborates.
They keep talking.
They fill the silence.
"Yeah, the project's been delayed because engineering won't commit to a timeline, and honestly I think it's because they're understaffed…"
and suddenly they're handing you gold, information you never would have gotten by asking a direct question.
A mirror says, "I'm listening, tell me more, help me understand," without you having to say any of those words.
It works because human beings have a deep need to feel connected and understood, and hearing your own words reflected back creates an instant, subconscious sense of "this person gets me."
The keys are: mirror the substance, not the trivia; use that inquisitive tone; and then, this is the hard part for most of us, shut up.
Stay silent and let the mirror do its work.
Silence after a mirror is where the magic lives.
And Voss tells the story of the very first time he used mirroring for real, and it's a doozy, because it was a disaster that still worked.
Nineteen-ninety-three, a bank robbery in Brooklyn, on a commercial street, and it goes wrong, turns into a hostage situation.
Voss is relatively new to hostage negotiation.
There are hostages inside, and on the phone is the ringleader, a smooth, calm-talking guy who's clearly running a game on them.
At one point the negotiators are led to believe there are multiple armed robbers inside.
And the lead robber tells Voss on the phone that his men want to let one of the hostages go, but there's a problem, they need a getaway car.
And Voss, not knowing what else to do, just mirrors him.
"A getaway car?
You guys wanted a getaway car?"
And the robber pauses, and starts explaining, and reveals more than he meant to.

Over hours of this, Voss and his team slowly used these techniques to unravel the situation, and it turned out the whole thing was less organized than it seemed, the leader was essentially alone in his control of it, and they got everyone out alive and the robbers into custody, no one killed.
That soft little tool, just echoing a man's own words back at him, kept a violent, chaotic standoff talking instead of shooting.
Voss walked away a believer.
Now, how you say all of this matters enormously, which brings us to the three voices.
Voss says a negotiator has essentially three tones of voice to choose from, and choosing the right one is like choosing the right club in golf.
The first and most powerful is what he calls the late-night FM DJ voice.
Think of that smooth, deep, slow, soothing radio voice that comes on at two in the morning.
Calm, warm, unhurried, with a downward inflection at the end of sentences, like you're gently laying the words down.
When you speak this way, you literally, physiologically, trigger a calming response in the other person's nervous system.
It signals, "I am in control, everything is going to be okay, we can slow down."
In a crisis it de-escalates.
In a business meeting it projects quiet authority.
That downward inflection is key, it makes a statement, it says, this is just how it is, calmly and without argument.
The second voice is the positive, playful voice.
And this should be your default, the one you use eighty, ninety percent of the time.
It's the voice of an easygoing, good-natured person with a genuine smile.
And here's a physical trick Voss swears by: actually smile while you talk, even on the phone where they can't see you.

The smile changes your tone, it comes through in your voice, and it makes the other person more relaxed, more collaborative, and literally quicker to solve problems, because a positive frame of mind makes people think faster and more creatively.
A smile is contagious even down a phone line.
The third voice is the direct or assertive voice.
And Voss basically says: avoid this one.
It's the hard, declarative, take-it-or-leave-it voice.
It almost always triggers pushback and defensiveness.
It says, "I'm the boss, do what I say," and people's hackles go straight up.
Use it rarely, if ever.
Most of the time, the FM DJ or the playful smile will get you far more.
And underpinning all of this is a rule Voss borrows and champions, the 7-38-55 rule.
It comes from research by Albert Mehrabian, and the idea is that in communicating feelings and attitudes, only about seven percent of a message is carried by the actual words.
Thirty-eight percent comes from your tone of voice.
And fifty-five percent comes from body language and facial expression.
Now, whether those exact percentages hold in every situation is debated, but the lesson is undeniable and profound: how you say something dwarfs what you say.
The words are the small part.
If someone's words say yes but their tone and body say no, believe the tone and the body.
And it means you should obsess over your tone at least as much as your script.
Now let's talk about what may be the most useful everyday tool in the entire book: labeling.
Labeling is the act of verbally identifying and naming the other person's emotion.
When you sense someone is feeling something, frustrated, worried, excited, skeptical, you name it out loud, and you do it with a specific gentle formula.
You start with, "It seems like…"
or "It sounds like…"
or "It looks like…"
So you say, "It seems like you're worried about the timeline."
"It sounds like this vendor really let you down before."
"It looks like something about this price is bothering you."
Notice what you are not saying.
You're never saying "I hear that you're worried," because the moment you say "I," you make it about you, and the other person gets defensive.
You keep yourself out of it entirely.
It seems like.
It sounds like.
It looks like.
And then, again, silence.
You label, and you wait.
Here's why labeling is so powerful.
Neuroscience shows that when you name a negative emotion, you actually reduce its intensity.
There's real brain-scan research on this, that putting a feeling into words takes some of the electrical charge out of the amygdala, the fear center of the brain.
So when you say to an angry person, "It seems like you're really upset about how this was handled," you're not inflaming their anger, you're defusing it.
You're helping them feel understood, and a person who feels understood calms down and opens up.
And labeling positive emotions amplifies them, reinforces them.
It's a tool that either takes the sting out of the bad or turns up the volume on the good.
The beautiful thing is that even if you name the emotion slightly wrong, the other person will correct you and explain, and now you understand them even better.
There's no way to lose.
And this brings us to one of the most gripping stories in the book, the one that shows off a tool Voss builds right on top of labeling, called the accusation audit.
Picture a standoff in Harlem.
Three fugitives, armed, wanted men, holed up in a high-rise apartment.
They've barricaded themselves in, and they're convinced that the second they open that door, the police are going to gun them down.
They won't answer the phone.
They won't respond to anything.
For six hours, Voss and his team try to make contact, and there is total silence from inside.
Dead air.
So Voss does something counterintuitive.
Instead of making promises or demands, he gets on the bullhorn and he starts naming, out loud, every single terrible thing those men are surely thinking about the police.
He does what he calls an accusation audit.
He says, in effect, "It looks like you don't want to come out.
It seems like you worry that if you open the door, we'll come in with guns blazing.
It looks like you don't want to go back to jail."
He voices all their fears and all their accusations against him and the police, before they can.
He takes every negative thing they might throw at him and he says it first, gently, in that calm voice.
An accusation audit is exactly that: you list, in advance, every accusation, every negative, every objection the other side might have against you, and you say them out loud yourself.
Because when you preempt someone's fears and criticisms by naming them first, you rob those fears of their power.
The person hears you acknowledging the worst, and they think, well, at least this guy gets it, at least he's not pretending.
And on that Harlem rooftop, after six hours of total silence, after Voss voiced all their fears back to them, the three fugitives eventually opened the door and surrendered peacefully.

Later, one of them said something Voss never forgot, that they came out because his voice, that calm, understanding voice that had named all their fears, made them feel like maybe they wouldn't be hurt, made them feel like they could trust the situation.
He talked them out with empathy.

In your life, an accusation audit sounds like starting a hard conversation with, "Look, you're probably going to think I'm being unreasonable here, and it may seem like I'm not valuing all the work your team put in…"
You disarm every objection before it can be fired at you.
Now here's where Voss really breaks from conventional wisdom, and it's the part that surprises everyone.
Every negotiation book, every sales trainer, every self-help guru tells you the goal is to get the other person to say yes.
Get them nodding.
Get to yes.
And Voss says that is exactly backwards.
He says the sweetest word in negotiation is not yes.
It's no.
Push for yes and you actually make people defensive, cornered, suspicious, because they know you're steering them.
But "no" makes people feel safe, in control, protected.
When someone says no, they feel like they still have their autonomy, and now, paradoxically, they relax and become willing to actually talk.
Voss breaks down that there are really three kinds of yes, and this is crucial.
There's the counterfeit yes, where someone says yes but they mean no, they're just saying it to get you off their back or to escape the conversation.
There's the confirmation yes, a simple reflexive answer to a straightforward question, yes the sky is blue, that commits to nothing.
And then there's the commitment yes, the real one, the yes that actually means action.
The problem is that people say the first two kinds constantly, and we mistake them for the third.
We hear yes and we think we've got a deal, and then it evaporates.
So instead of chasing those slippery yeses, Voss says, invite the no.
Make it safe for people to say no, because a no gives you real information and a real starting point.
And he gives you the single most magical email he knows, the no-oriented question that gets responses when nothing else will.
When someone has gone dark on you, stopped answering, ghosting you, you send them one line: "Have you given up on this project?"
Or, "Have you given up on getting this done?"
And Voss says the reply rate on that is astonishing.
Because "have you given up" triggers that loss aversion, that fear of losing something, and it lets them reclaim control by saying, "No!
I haven't given up, I've just been swamped, here's where we are…"
That little no unlocks the whole conversation.
It's the opposite of "Do you have a few minutes to talk?" which feels like a trap.
Have you given up?" feels like freedom.
So flip your questions.
Instead of "Does this work for you?" try "Is this a bad idea?"
Instead of "Do you agree?" try "Are you against this?"
Let them protect themselves with a no, and watch them open up.
And this leads to a distinction that Voss says is one of the most important in the whole book, and it's about two little phrases that sound almost identical but are worlds apart.
When you're trying to persuade someone, when you want to know you've truly gotten through, the words you are listening for are "that's right."
Not "you're right."
"That's right."
There's a huge difference.
When someone says "you're right," they are usually blowing you off.
Think about it, when your spouse is nagging you, what do you say to make them stop?
"You're right, you're right."
It's dismissive, it means "please stop talking now, I'll agree just to end this."
"You're right" is what people say to get you to shut up and go away.
But "that's right" is completely different.

"That's right" is what someone says when they feel that you have truly, deeply understood them, when you've articulated their own situation back to them better than they could themselves, and something clicks, and they feel seen.
That's the breakthrough moment.
That's when real influence happens, because now they own the conclusion, it feels like theirs.
So how do you make someone say "that's right"?
You build toward it with a summary.

A great summary is a combination of labeling their emotions and paraphrasing their world, woven together and reflected back so completely and accurately that the only possible response is "that's right."
You listen and listen, and then you say, "So let me make sure I understand.

It sounds like you've been burned by vendors before who overpromised, it seems like what you really need this time is someone who'll be straight with you about the risks, and it looks like the price matters less to you than knowing you can trust the person you're working with.
Is that right?"
And when they say, softly, "Yeah… that's right," you've won.
Not the argument, the relationship.
Now they're on your side.

Voss illustrates this with a hostage story where getting the captor to say "that's right" was the turning point that saved lives, where a long, patient summary of the man's grievances and fears finally landed and he exhaled and said "that's right," and from that moment he was workable.
Chase "that's right."
Never be satisfied with "you're right."
Okay, now we get into the more assertive, deal-shaping side of things, what Voss calls bending their reality.
Once you've built that connection and trust, you start to reshape how the other person sees the deal itself, using the levers of human psychology.
The first lever is the word "fair."
Voss calls it the F-word, and he says it's the most powerful, and most sneakily dangerous, word in a negotiation.
Because "fair" hits people right in the gut, emotionally.

When someone tells you, "I just want what's fair," or "Let's be fair here," or "We're offering you a fair deal," watch out, because usually they're using it to put you on the defensive, to make you feel that if you push back, you're being unreasonable.
It's an emotional jab dressed up as reason.
And Voss says the way to handle it is beautiful.
When someone throws "fair" at you as a weapon, you don't get defensive, you calmly say, "I want you to feel like you're being treated fairly at all times.
So please, stop me at any point where you feel I'm being unfair, and we'll address it."
You turn it around, you disarm it, you put the accusation to rest and take back control, all while sounding like the most reasonable person in the room.
The next lever is anchoring, and specifically anchoring emotions before numbers.

In most cases, Voss actually advises letting the other side go first on price when you have no idea what the item is worth, or when the range is wildly uncertain, because they might surprise you and their number might be better than what you'd have proposed.
But when you do anchor, you anchor the emotions first.
Before you talk numbers, you prime them with an accusation audit, something like, "I'm going to give you a number, and it's going to seem really low, and honestly you might think I'm crazy…"
Now you've braced them for a low number, so when it lands it feels less shocking.
You set the emotional stage before you set the price.
Then there's loss aversion, which is that deep human truth that we hate losing something far more than we love gaining the equivalent.
It's roughly a two-to-one thing, the pain of a loss dwarfs the pleasure of a gain.
So a savvy negotiator frames things in terms of what the other person stands to lose by not doing the deal, not just what they stand to gain.
Instead of "Here's how much you'll save," you say, "I'd hate for you to lose the position you've built" or "If we can't figure this out, here's what you're going to miss."
Loss looms larger, and it moves people.
And Voss reminds you to always look for the non-monetary.
Not everything is about the money on the table.
Sometimes what the other side truly wants is recognition, or security, or to look good to their boss, or to feel respected, or a favor down the road, or their name on something.
If you can give them something that costs you little but means a lot to them emotionally, you can bridge gaps that money alone never could.
The whole point of discovery is finding those hidden currencies.
Now, the workhorse tool for actually bending reality and getting the other side to solve your problems for you: calibrated questions.
These are open-ended questions carefully designed to give the other person the illusion of control while actually steering the conversation exactly where you want it.
And the golden rule is: they start with "how" or "what."
Almost never "why."
Why puts people on the defensive in every language on earth, "why did you do that" feels like an accusation.
But "how" and "what" invite the person to think, to explain, to open up, and here's the sneaky part, to work on your problem for you.
The single most powerful calibrated question Voss teaches, the one that echoes back to that Harvard role-play with Mnookin, is this: "How am I supposed to do that?"

Somebody makes a demand you can't meet, a kidnapper demands a million dollars you don't have, a client demands a price you can't hit, and instead of saying no, instead of arguing, you say, in that calm FM DJ voice, "How am I supposed to do that?"
And what happens is beautiful.
You've said no without saying no.
You haven't refused, you haven't fought, you've simply handed the problem back to them and made them figure out how to solve it.
Now they have to look at your constraints.
They have to become your partner in finding a path.
It forces them to consider your situation and often to talk themselves down from their own demand.
Other great calibrated questions: "What about this is important to you?"
"How can we solve this problem?"
"What are we trying to accomplish here?"
"What's the biggest challenge you face?"
"How would you like me to proceed?"
Each one gets them talking, gives you information, and makes them feel in charge while you steer.
There's also the Rule of Three, which shows up in a couple of places, and here's one of them: get the other person to agree to something three times in the same conversation.
The first is the commitment, the second and third you circle back with calibrated questions and labels, "How will we know it's working?"
"What happens if we hit a snag?"
And by making them affirm it three ways, you flush out any counterfeit yes, because it's really hard to keep faking sincerity three times.
Triple confirmation exposes the lie or cements the truth.
And Voss shows all of this working in the Haiti kidnappings.
This is real, harrowing stuff.

In Haiti, kidnapping for ransom was practically an industry, and Voss and his colleagues would get on the phone with kidnappers who'd snatched someone's family member and were demanding, say, a hundred and fifty thousand dollars, and the family had nowhere near that.
And instead of haggling or threatening, Voss would deploy these gentle, calibrated questions.
"How am I supposed to come up with that?"
"How do I know my loved one is even safe?"
"What are we going to do if I can't get that kind of money?"

And time after time, through nothing but patient questions and calm empathy, those enormous ransom demands would collapse, sometimes down to a tiny fraction, a few thousand dollars, sometimes practically to nothing, and the hostages came home.

Voss discovered along the way that many of these kidnappings were about a specific, immediate need, the kidnappers wanted party money for the weekend, cash in hand, and they weren't going to hurt anyone, so the calm questions that slowed everything down and reframed the whole thing worked.
Gentle questions dismantled demands that threats never could have.
Now, once you're close to a deal, you have to make sure it actually gets done, and you have to be able to spot when someone's lying to you.
Voss gives you tools for both.
For ensuring execution, calibrated questions again, "How will we know we're on track?"
"What happens if we get behind?"
You make the other side describe the implementation in their own words, so they own it, so it's their plan, not just yours imposed on them, because people follow through on their own plans.
For spotting liars, Voss points to a few tells.

One is what he calls, borrowing from research, the Pinocchio effect: liars tend to use more words and more third-person pronouns, they over-explain, they work too hard to convince you, because deep down they want to distance themselves from the lie and prove they're being honest.
The truth is usually simpler and shorter.
When someone floods you with justification, be alert.
The Rule of Three shows up here too, if you're unsure whether someone means what they say, get them to say it three times, and watch it crack under repetition if it's false.
And then there's this lovely, humane tool: use their name.
Voss calls it the Chris discount.

He tells a story of being at a bar, or in various situations, where he'd introduce himself by name, "Hi, I'm Chris," personalize the interaction, use the other person's name warmly, and he'd end up getting a better deal, a discount, a favor, simply because he'd turned a transaction into a human relationship.
When you use someone's name, sincerely and warmly, you make them feel valued as a person, and it becomes much harder for them to be harsh or dishonest with you.
So drop your name, learn theirs, use it.
It humanizes everything.
Okay, now the crown jewel of the tactical side of the book, the part everyone wants to know about: the Ackerman bargaining system.

This is Voss's specific, battle-tested method for haggling over a number, and it's brilliant because it's systematic, it removes the emotion and the guesswork, and it uses human psychology at every step.
It's named after Mike Ackerman, and here's how it works.
First, before you do anything, you set your target price, the goal, the number you actually want to end up at.
Let's say you're negotiating a price and your target, the most you're willing to pay, is a hundred dollars.
Everything is calculated as a percentage of that target.
Your first offer is sixty-five percent of your target.
So you open at sixty-five dollars.
That's your anchor, deliberately low, but not insultingly, absurdly low, low enough to give you room.
Then, and this is important, you make your increases in decreasing increments.
Your next offer, only after using empathy and calibrated questions to get them to respond, is eighty-five percent, so eighty-five dollars.
Then your third offer is ninety-five percent, ninety-five dollars.
And your final offer is one hundred percent of your target, but with a twist, you don't say a round hundred.
Watch the increments shrink: from sixty-five you jumped twenty to eighty-five, then ten to ninety-five, then a small final move.
That shrinking pattern sends a powerful subconscious message, that you are scraping the very bottom of the barrel, that you're maxed out, that there's almost nothing left to give.
Each smaller step says, "I'm reaching my limit."
Now several genius details.
Between each of your offers, you don't just throw out the next number.
You use those calibrated questions and labels to make them feel the squeeze and to try to get them to bid against themselves.
You say, "How am I supposed to go any higher than that?"
You make them work for every dollar.
And you use the accusation audit and empathy so it never feels like a fight.
Then, on your final number, instead of a round hundred dollars, you use a precise, oddly specific number.
Not a hundred, but ninety-seven dollars and sixty-three cents.
Why?
Because a precise number sounds like it was carefully calculated, like it's the real, thought-through, bottom-line figure, not something you just made up or can easily move off of.
Round numbers feel soft and negotiable, ninety-seven sixty-three feels final, like it's tied to some real constraint.
It carries weight and credibility.
And then the final flourish, the cherry on top: at the end, along with your last offer, you throw in a small non-cash gift.
Something unrelated to the money, a little extra, a token.
In a business deal it might be, "And I'll throw in some tickets, or I'll include the extended warranty, or we'll cover the shipping."

That little gift, offered right at the close, makes the other person feel like they got a bonus, like you went out of your way for them, and it dramatically increases their sense that this was a fair, generous deal, so they say yes.
It signals goodwill and it makes your final number feel like the true end of the road.
Let me walk you through it whole.
Say you're buying a used car listed at eleven thousand dollars, and you've decided your target, the most you'll pay, is nine thousand.
You open with an accusation audit: "Look, I'm going to give you a number and you're probably going to think it's way too low, and honestly you might be a little offended, but hear me out."
Then you anchor at sixty-five percent of nine thousand, so about fifty-eight fifty.
The seller balks.
You don't budge on emotion, you ask, "It seems like that's disappointing.
Help me understand, how did you arrive at eleven thousand?"
You let them talk.
Then you move to eighty-five percent, seventy-six fifty, and you say, "How am I supposed to go higher than that, given what I've got?"
They push.
You go to ninety-five percent, eighty-five fifty.
Notice the increments shrinking, you're signaling you're near your limit.
Then your final offer, one hundred percent of target, but precise, eight thousand nine hundred and thirty-five dollars, said like it's every last cent you have.
And you add, "And you know what, I'll drive out to you and handle the paperwork transfer myself, save you the hassle."
That's your non-cash gift.
That whole sequence, calm, empathetic, systematic, will land you your deal far more often than blurting out a number and splitting the difference.
Now, finally, we come to the deepest, most advanced idea in the book, the one Voss saves for last and calls the black swans.
A black swan is a piece of information you don't know exists, an unknown unknown, that once discovered completely changes the negotiation.
Before Europeans reached Australia, everyone "knew" all swans were white, it was a synonym for certainty, and then they found black swans, and one discovery shattered a universal belief.

In every negotiation, Voss says, there are three to five pieces of information, black swans, that if you knew them would change everything, that would give you enormous leverage or reveal that everything you assumed was wrong.
The master negotiator is a hunter of black swans.
And to understand why he takes this so seriously, you have to hear the tragedy that taught him, the William Griffin case.
This was a bank robbery and hostage situation, and it broke every assumption the FBI held.
See, hostage negotiators operated on a foundational belief, an unspoken certainty: that hostage-takers want to live.
The entire playbook rested on it.
As long as the person inside wants to survive, you have leverage, you can trade, you can wait them out, time is on your side.
But William Griffin was different.
He'd already killed people before the police even arrived.
And during the standoff, at a certain point, Griffin walked a hostage out into plain view and, in front of the officers, forced a confrontation that got himself shot and killed by police.
It turned out he had no intention of surviving.
He wanted to die, and he'd orchestrated the whole thing, using the hostages, to make it happen, essentially suicide by cop, and he murdered a hostage in the process.
It was horrifying, and it shattered the assumption that had underpinned everything.
There had been a black swan the whole time, the fact that this man wanted to die, and because no one knew it, every standard tactic was not just useless but dangerous.
That tragedy taught Voss that you must never assume you know what's driving the other person.
There is always something you don't know, and it might be the most important thing.
So how do you hunt black swans?
Leverage, first, and Voss says there are three kinds.
There's positive leverage, which is your ability to give the other person something they want, to provide what they need.
There's negative leverage, your ability to make them suffer a loss, the threat of taking something away, and Voss says use this carefully, it's powerful but it can blow up the relationship.
And there's normative leverage, which is using the other person's own norms, values, and standards against them.
If someone prides themselves on being fair, or on being a person of their word, you can hold them to their own stated standards.
"You've always told me you value long-term partnerships, so it seems like this would be a chance to prove that."
You use what they believe about themselves.

And the practical way to unearth black swans is to keep the other person talking and listen for what doesn't fit, watch for the things they say that seem strange or contradictory, because those are the clues.
Get face-to-face when you can, because black swans slip out in unguarded moments, in body language, in the little aside comments.

Notice when someone's behavior seems irrational, because it's almost never actually irrational, it just means you're missing information, you don't yet know the constraint or the value or the fear that makes their behavior make perfect sense to them.
When someone seems crazy, they're not crazy, they're constrained by something you can't see, or misinformed, or they have interests you haven't discovered.
And the great open question to go hunting with is, "What's the biggest challenge you face?" or "What are you up against here?"
Because the answer to that question so often reveals the hidden thing, the real pressure, the black swan, that once you know it, lets you craft an offer they can't refuse.
So let me pull this whole thing together for you, because it's a system, and it flows.
You begin by throwing out the fantasy that people are rational, and you replace it with tactical empathy, the discipline of truly understanding the other person's emotional world so you can guide it.
You use mirroring, just repeating their last few words, to keep them talking and reveal information, all in that warm, curious tone.

You choose your voice deliberately, the calm late-night FM DJ voice to soothe and take control, the smiling playful voice as your default, and you almost never reach for the hard assertive voice, remembering that how you say it matters far more than the words, seven percent words, thirty-eight percent tone, fifty-five percent body.
You label their emotions out loud, it seems like, it sounds like, it looks like, to defuse the negatives and reinforce the positives and make them feel seen.

When they're hostile or resistant, you run an accusation audit, saying all their objections and fears yourself before they can, the way Voss talked three armed fugitives off a Harlem rooftop with nothing but understanding.

You stop chasing yes and you make it safe for them to say no, because no makes people feel in control and willing to engage, and you keep a dead deal alive with that magic line, "Have you given up on this?"
You work patiently toward the words "that's right," building an airtight summary of their world that makes them exhale and agree, and you never settle for the dismissive "you're right."

Then you start to bend their reality, handling the loaded word "fair" by inviting them to stop you anywhere they feel mistreated, anchoring emotions before numbers, framing things around what they stand to lose, and hunting for the non-monetary currencies that mean everything to them and cost you little.

You steer with calibrated how and what questions, above all "How am I supposed to do that?", the question that hands your problem back to them and makes them your partner, the same gentle questions that collapsed impossible ransom demands in Haiti.
You confirm sincerity and follow-through with the Rule of Three, you spot the liar who uses too many words, and you humanize every exchange by using names, the Chris discount.

When it's time to talk numbers, you don't wing it and you never just split the difference, you run the Ackerman system, target first, then offers at sixty-five, eighty-five, ninety-five, and one hundred percent, in shrinking increments that scream you're at your limit, capped with a precise, oddly specific final number and a small unexpected gift.

And through all of it, you stay humble and curious, hunting for the black swans, the unknown unknowns, because William Griffin taught the FBI in the worst way possible that the thing you don't know can be the only thing that matters, and that when someone seems irrational, you're simply missing the information that would make them make sense.
Here's the heart of it, the thing Voss most wants you to carry.
Negotiation is not about beating the other person.
It's not a war to be won with clever arguments or hard tactics.

It is, at its core, an act of collaboration and discovery, the work of understanding another human being so deeply that you can find a way forward that works for both of you, and often a way forward that's far better than the compromise you'd have settled for.
The most powerful force you can bring to any table is not aggression or leverage or a killer opening line.
It's empathy.

It's the willingness to genuinely listen, to make the other person feel heard and safe and respected, because a person who feels understood is a person who becomes reasonable, generous, and willing to move.

That FBI agent talking to a desperate man on a bridge, and you asking your boss for a raise, and you buying a car, and you working through a hard moment with someone you love, you are all doing the same essential thing.
You're trying to reach another human heart.
So the next time you're across the table from someone, resist that urge to split the difference and call it fair.
Slow down.
Get curious.
Ask "How am I supposed to do that?"
Label what they're feeling.
Chase that quiet "that's right."
Hunt for the thing you don't yet know.
And you'll find that the surest way to get what you want is, first, to make the other person feel truly, completely understood.
That's the whole secret.
That's the book.
Now go use it.
